persons.arguments_against: Richards is the commonest surname in this batch (86 officials, 38 bios), so each stint must survive the namesake test independently. North Borneo: could the 1933 'A. F. Richards, Governor, C.M.G.' be another A. F. Richards? The bio gives 'gov', N. Borneo, Mar., 1930' and 'C.M.G. (1933)' — the unique governorship plus the honour appearing in the staff list in exactly its award year. Two A. F. Richardses with C.M.G.s both governing North Borneo is not credible; the position_sim FAIL is the OCR contraction 'gov'' vs 'Governor'. The stint's nominal 1933–1948 span outlasting his Nov. 1933 departure to Gambia is explained by the rows themselves: the 1936 row sits in a 'Governors' (roll of governors) department with no current position, the standard retrospective listing trap, which proves listing-not-currency but does not change whose stint it is. Jamaica: could a relative be the 1940 'Sir Arthur F. Richards'? The office 'Captain-General and Governor-in-Chief' is unique to Jamaica's governor, the bio gives 'capt.-gen. and gov'-in-ch., Jamaica, 1938', and the staff-list honour K.C.M.G. matches the bio's 'K.C.M.G. (1935)' with the style 'Sir'. Both counter-hypotheses fail against unique offices with date and honour agreement.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second A. F. Richards governing North Borneo c. 1930–1933 or Jamaica c. 1938–1940, or a source showing the 1936/1940/1948 North Borneo rows belong to a later, different Governor Richards rather than a retrospective governors' roll.

notes: Both stints are unique gubernatorial offices matching the bio's career sequence (N. Borneo 1930-33, then Gambia, Fiji, Jamaica 1938) with honours appearing in staff lists in the right years (C.M.G. 1933; K.C.M.G./'Sir' by 1940). The North Borneo stint's post-1933 editions are retrospective 'Governors' roll entries, consistent with the same person.
